problem_statement: Conditional diffusion models can be guided using a separately trained classifier (classifier guidance), but this requires training an additional model on noisy data and introduces a trade-off between sample quality and diversity. We want conditional generation with strong adherence to the conditioning signal WITHOUT needing a separate classifier. How can we achieve classifier-like guidance using only the diffusion model itself?
known_ingredients: Classifier guidance works by adding the gradient of a classifier's log-probability to the diffusion score function at each sampling step
A conditional diffusion model p(x|c) and an unconditional model p(x) are related by Bayes' rule: the classifier gradient is proportional to the difference between conditional and unconditional scores
Training a separate classifier on noisy data at every noise level is expensive and introduces its own failure modes
Conditional and unconditional diffusion models can be trained as a single neural network by randomly dropping the conditioning signal during training
The guidance scale parameter in classifier guidance controls the quality-diversity trade-off
key_insight: You don't need a separate classifier at all. Since the classifier gradient is proportional to (conditional score - unconditional score), you can directly compute guidance by taking the difference between the conditional and unconditional predictions of a single model trained with random conditioning dropout. Extrapolating this difference with a scale factor > 1 gives classifier-free guidance that matches or exceeds classifier guidance quality.
insight_components: The classifier can be implicitly represented as the difference between conditional and unconditional diffusion model scores
A single model can serve as both conditional and unconditional by dropping conditioning during training
Extrapolating the difference (scale > 1) amplifies the conditioning signal analogously to classifier guidance temperature
difficulty_rationale: The mathematical relationship is almost directly stated in the ingredients (Bayes' rule connection). The key leap is realizing you can use the same model for both terms and then extrapolate.
